clinical_history: 18-year-old pre-op ECG for knee surgery. What does this ECG show?
diagnosis: Limb lead reversal. The left and right arm leads are reversed. The clue is inversion of the P wave and QRS complex in lead I. Whenever you see a negative P wave and QRS complex in lead I the likely diagnosis is limb lead reversal. Dextrocardia is another possibility, but in dextrocardia there is loss of R wave progression in the left chest leads  which is not seen in this tracing. Another clue is the dissimilarity of the morphology of the QRS complexes in lead I and V6. No evidence of multiple P wave morphologies are present ruling out MAT.(The variability in heart rate here is due to respiratory sinus arrhythmia, a physiologic finding that is most apparent in younger healthy subjects.) Also, note the slight J point/ST elevations in leads with a positive QRS, consistent with normal variant (benign) early repolarization pattern.

report.rate: Approx. 75 bpm (variable due to respiratory sinus arrhythmia)
report.axis: Apparent Right Axis Deviation (Lead I negative, aVF positive)
report.intervals: PR: ~160 ms, QRS: ~80 ms, QTc: ~450 ms
report.findings: Inverted P wave, QRS complex, and T wave in lead I; dissimilarity of QRS morphology between leads I and V6; normal R wave progression in precordial leads; slight J-point and ST-segment elevations consistent with benign early repolarization.
report.impression: Limb lead reversal (Left Arm/Right Arm); normal variant early repolarization; respiratory sinus arrhythmia.
